content:
Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor Francesca Passaro a , Marcello Pagliero a,b,*

, Ilaria Rizzardi a, Antonio Comite a,b a Membrane&membrane Research Group, Department of Chemistry and Industrial Chemistry, University of Genoa, Via Dodecaneso 31, 16146, Genoa, Italyb INSTM, Udr di Genova, Via Dodecaneso 31, 16146, Genoa, Italy

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Braided membranesDip-coatingNonsolvent induced phase separationMembrane aerated biological reactorWastewater treatment

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: A B S T R A C T In this work, hollow fibre polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) supported membranes were prepared by dip coating a fibre glass sleeve in a PVDF solution, followed by precipitation via nonsolvent induced phase separation (NIPS) in 96% ethanol. The combination of these was optimized for obtaining membranes with a low-resistance thin polymeric layer and enhanced mechanical stability. The influence of both the withdrawal rate and the viscosity of the dope solution were investigated. The thickness of the PVDF coating layer was evaluated in the frame of the Landau-Levich model, which predicts that the film thickness results from the balance between viscous drag and surface tension forces. Among all the prepared membranes, the membranes prepared from a dope solution at 60 ◦C and with a withdrawal rate of 0.5 cm/s were selected to be used as support to grow biofilm for an application as Membrane Aerated Biological Reactor (MABR). The performance of the MABR, with the biofilm grown on the outer surface of the PVDF membranes and airflow supplied through the lumen of the fibre glass sleeve, was evaluated in terms of removal of Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), ammoniacal nitrogen and total nitrogen. The MABR set up enabled the simultaneous abatement of the aforementioned parameters, with 78% of removal for COD, 21% for ammonia and 5% for total nitrogen, an interesting starting point for lab-made membranes exploited for this application.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: 1. Introduction In membrane contactors, the membrane provides a well-defined interface for mass transfer between two different phases, which may be both liquid or a liquid phase and a gaseous one [1]. Such type of membranes can find an application in membrane aerated bioreactor (MABR). MABR is a novel technology that has caught increasing interest in the last decades, thanks to the fact that it can combine the advantage of a simultaneous removal of organic matter and nitrogen, with the possibility to grow a biomass on an active support. If the adhesion to the support is efficient, the energy cost for the separation of the biomass from the treated wastewater is significantly reduced [2]. In such a counter diffusional regime, where oxygen can be supplied from the lumen side of the hollow fibre membrane and the pollutants are supplied from the external aqueous bulk, a diversification of biomass species can be achieved, and multiple abatements can be observed [3]. Poly­vinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is a thermoplastic polymer, water insoluble, with good chemical and mechanical resistance and good processability, making it suitable for various applications. However, its hydrophobicity and microporous structure, allow for consistent biofouling and pore clogging, which can be an important drawback for membrane transport phenomena [2,4]. Consequently, PVDF can be manufactured as a microporous hollow fibre membrane for its simultaneous use as a bio­film growth support and oxygen supplier.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: nsequently, PVDF can be manufactured as a microporous hollow fibre membrane for its simultaneous use as a bio­film growth support and oxygen supplier. Polymeric membranes are widely applied in water and wastewater treatment, desalination, gas separation, and biomedical devices, due to their efficiency, cost-effectiveness, process modularity. Nevertheless, their practical application often faces significant challenges, particularly concerning mechanical stability, fouling resistance, and long-term operational durability. Therefore, continuous advancements in mem­brane development are still necessary, especially when the membrane geometry is tubular or hollow fibre. One promising approach for the tube-like polymeric membranes is the incorporation of a braided support structure, which can significantly enhance their mechanical robustness and functional performance [5]. As a matter of fact, braided support structures significantly

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: Available online 2 February 2026 0376-7388/ 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). contribute to enhancing membrane performance and characteristics in various ways. A braided support can provide very high mechanical stability and resistance to deformation, rupture, and compaction under high pressure or prolonged usage. Therefore, a braided support can provide to the polymeric membrane structural integrity over longer operational times [5,6]. The open, highly permeable structure of braided supports allows the fluid permeating through the polymeric membrane to be quickly removed. This can lead to more efficient separation processes and reduce energy consumption. Also, the unique geometry of braided supports can help mitigate fouling by promoting turbulent flow and preventing the accumulation of fouling agents on the membrane surface [7]. This reduces the frequency and intensity of cleaning procedures, prolonging the membrane's operational lifespan and maintaining its performance over time [8–10]. Overall, the integration of braided support structures into membrane systems offers numerous benefits, resulting in improved membranes suitable for a wide range of applications [7]. Among the various mate­rials used for commercial braided supports, glass fibre is commonly used for their mechanical and chemical resistance usual for electric and thermal applications.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: or commercial braided supports, glass fibre is commonly used for their mechanical and chemical resistance usual for electric and thermal applications. The braided supports are commercially available both as plane clothes and as sleeves. Moreover, braided sleeves can be found in different diameters from few millimetres to centimetres. Another reason for using a support is evident in the preparation phase of the membrane: without it, the porous structure can collapse on itself during the drying process, resulting in a membrane with reduced porosity. The effect of the support on the final structure of membranes has been thoroughly investigated [11,12]. The successful development of polymeric membranes supported on braided supports, involves the selection of the polymer for the selective layer and of a compatible braided material, optimization the operating condition for the membrane deposition on the braided support [13]. Phase inversion, interfacial polymerization, and electrospinning tech­niques are usually used to prepare the membrane layer onto the braided support, each offering distinct advantages in terms of membrane morphology and performance. Dip coating is a favoured technique for developing polymer mem­branes supported by braided materials. This method involves immersing a support into a solution containing the coating materials and then withdrawing it at a controlled speed.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: ed materials. This method involves immersing a support into a solution containing the coating materials and then withdrawing it at a controlled speed. As the substrate is pulled, a coating film forms due to a combination of hydrodynamic and evaporation ef­fects [14]. The coating process can be explained in three steps: as the substrate is pulled off, a hydrodynamic boundary layer forms between the entrained liquid, responsible for the formation of the coating, and the bulk solution, then the solvent is either removed with evaporation or with substitution with nonsolvent, depositing the coating material on the substrate. The thickness of the coating is influenced by various factors, including the withdrawal speed, solution viscosity, concentra­tion, and evaporation rate. Dip coating can be affected by several issues, such as impurities, precursor aggregation, air bubbles, and substrate irregularities. The choice of substrate is crucial, requiring chemical compatibility to enhance the adhesion of the coating [15]. This technique can produce membrane layers with thicknesses ranging from tenth to hundreds of microns, making it suitable for creating additional layers with pore sizes in the micropore (1–2 nm), mesopore (2–50 nm), and macropore (50 nm - 5 μm) ranges [16]. The minimum coating thickness should ideally be comparable to the sub­strate maximum surface roughness to avoid defects.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: e (50 nm - 5 μm) ranges [16]. The minimum coating thickness should ideally be comparable to the sub­strate maximum surface roughness to avoid defects. Unlike spin coating, dip coating typically produces layer thicknesses much larger than the pore sizes of the dipped material. Therefore, the added layer must be inherently porous or have porosity created through techniques like phase separation, crystallization, or etching [17,18]. To trigger the precipitation of the depositing layer, instead of a thermal evaporation, a nonsolvent induced phase inversion (NIPS) can be used. NIPS is a synthesis technique used to produce asymmetric polymer membranes for separation processes, both in laboratory and industrial scale. The mechanism of membrane formation by NIPS con­sists in the precipitation of a solution made of a polymer in a suitable solvent. The solidification occurs when the dissolved polymer is put into a nonsolvent bath, where the exchange between solvent and nonsolvent is triggered [19,20]. The liquid-liquid demixing that occurs in the NIPS process and the structure of the membrane are strongly influenced by the thermodynamics and kinetics of the system. The final morphology of the membrane is defined by the distribution and shape of the pores created in the NIPS process [21]. As much as this work is concerned, it was decided to prepare hollow fibre PVDF membranes supported on a braided material.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: ed in the NIPS process [21]. As much as this work is concerned, it was decided to prepare hollow fibre PVDF membranes supported on a braided material. The deposition was carried out through dip coating of a PVDF solution on braided sleeve. Then a phase separation resulting in a porous membrane layer on the outer surface of the braided sleeve was triggered by immersion of the supported polymeric layer into a nonsolvent. A similar type of mem­branes was already developed in a previous work [22] where it was used in a membrane distillation setup. To improve the permeability of this kind of membranes, it was decided to change the nonsolvent, exploiting the findings of tests performed on flat sheet membranes in another previous investigation where, using a weak nonsolvent such as ethanol, it was possible to delay the precipitation process, obtaining a higher surface porosity and hydrophobicity [23]. Also, the braided sleeves used in this work had a smaller diameter than in the first work, so that the packing density of the membrane module could be increased. As a matter of fact, the purpose of the preparation of this type of membranes was to be set in a MABR, in which the PVDF membranes would be the support for the bacterial biofilm growth.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: the preparation of this type of membranes was to be set in a MABR, in which the PVDF membranes would be the support for the bacterial biofilm growth. In this kind of set up, the membranes were aerated on the lumen side, to supply oxygen to the bacterial cells of the biofilm, allowing the cells closer to the support to have an aerobic metabolism and properly lower the COD of a synthetic wastewater by oxidating the pollutants. In fact, a better packing can be achieved by reducing the diameter of the hollow fibre membranes to meet the typical diameter of hollow fi­bres in order to increase their number in the same volume of the module of the reactor. Consequently, a higher external surface provides more available space for biofilm growth, which determines a more efficient removal of pollutants from water. Specifically, in this work the effect of two parameters of the dip coating technique were explored: the viscosity of the polymeric solution and the withdrawal rate. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Membrane preparation A certain amount of PVDF (Solef 6010, Solvay Speciality Polymers – Italy, Mw 300 kDa) was dissolved in triethyl phosphate (TEP, Merck – Germany) at 70 ◦C under stirring for 8 h, in order to obtain the dope solutions for PVDF-based membranes. Based on previous works [24], a PVDF concentration of 14.5 wt% was selected to obtain the desired pore size.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: dope solutions for PVDF-based membranes. Based on previous works [24], a PVDF concentration of 14.5 wt% was selected to obtain the desired pore size. Once the complete PVDF dissolution was achieved, the solution was placed in an oven set at the desired temperature (30–70 ◦C) for 1 h to reach the deposition conditions and to remove the air bubbles trapped during the stirring process. Then the membrane was prepared by the dip coating technique exploiting the nonsolvent induced phase separation process, using the equipment schematized in Fig. 1. A braided glass fibre sleeve (BP-GV-10 M from Balconi Srl, 0.2 mm of thickness, 3.5 mm of external diameter, Fig. 2) was placed on a straight stainless-steel rod that acted as solid support during the coating process (A). The open, permeable structure of this support oppose little to none resistance to mass transfer, as the permeability of supported membrane is mostly related to the thickness of the selective layer and to its porosity [25]. The steel rod was hung to a horizontal shaft (B) that was connected to a gear motor (D) with a cable and an adequate series of pulleys. The moving bar was lowered to its bottom position, careful to have the first part of the braided support through the hole of a 10 mL solution container (C).

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: Once the setup was ready, a small amount of dope solution (approximately 5 mL) was poured into the solution container, and the braided support was raised at the required speed. With this kind of system, the rod with the support enters the liquid from the bottom part of the container and moves upwards. So, the contact time between the support and the polymeric solution is similar for all the section of the length of the sleeve and this can avoid the formation of big droplets of deposited material on the bottom part. The withdrawal speed was controlled using an external motor driver that allowed to vary the speed in the range 0.5–1.6 cm/s. As soon as the deposition was completed, the support rod was detached from the coating system and immersed in ethanol to complete the NIPS process. After 2 h, the support rod was removed, and the membrane was thoroughly washed with deionized water in order to remove any trace of ethanol or TEP that could have been left and finally dried at room temperature overnight before pro­ceeding with any characterization. Eventually, since there was some accumulation of deposited polymer on the very bottom part of the support, due to a gravitational effect, it was decided to cut a small section of this part, of about 1.5 cm. This effect was more appreciable at higher temperatures, when the viscosity is lower and the polymer can be dragged along the length of the tubular support.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: ffect was more appreciable at higher temperatures, when the viscosity is lower and the polymer can be dragged along the length of the tubular support. Except for this minor issue, the thickness of the top part and the bottom part did not differ for more than 5% with this setup. 2.2. Membrane characterization The PVDF solution viscosity was measured using a Brookfield DV II + viscometer at different temperatures using a heating jacket for the sample container. The Liquid-Liquid Displacement Porometry (LLDP) was chosen as the method to evaluate the mean pore size of the mem­branes. To this end, a porometer built in the laboratory and previously described elsewhere [26] was used. Water and 1-octanol were chosen as displacing and wetting liquid respectively (interfacial tension of 1-octanol/water = 8.5 mN/m, at 20 ◦C [27]). First, the membrane was immersed in octanol overnight and then 76 mm2 of membrane was inserted the LLDP test cell. To perform the measurement, water was forced through the membrane using a precision syringe pump (Tele­dyne, ISCO 260D) and for each set flowrate the equilibrium pressure was registered. The flowrate was increased stepwise until an almost linear pressure increase was observed. For each membrane, three different samples were tested. The membranes were observed using a Carl Zeiss AG - SUPRA 40VP field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM).

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: ree different samples were tested. The membranes were observed using a Carl Zeiss AG - SUPRA 40VP field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM). For the observa­tion of the membrane surface morphology, a membrane sample was simply mounted on an aluminium stub and coated with a carbon nanofilm before observation. The cross sections of the membranes were obtained by fracturing the sample in liquid nitrogen. The hydrophobicity was assessed using a digital optical tensiometer (Biolin Scientific, Attension Theta). A 3 μL droplet was automatically dispensed onto the membrane surface using a syringe. For each sample,

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: 3 droplets were analysed in different spots recording a 10 s video at 15 frame per second; the contact angle was then automatically measured in each frame. 2.3. MABR construction and performance evaluation First, a small sample of biomass, obtained from a conventional activated sludge (CAS) depuration plant and containing microorganisms of various kinds (e.g.: rotifers, ciliated organisms, nitrobacter and nitrosomonas) was grown in the laboratory in a traditional batch reactor, up to a concentration of 10 g/L. The module for the MABR was built using 8 PVDF membranes pre­pared as described in the previous sections. First, a polyethylene (PE) mask, suitable to precisely arrange the membranes and a central sup­porting rod, was prepared drilling the required holes in adequate posi­tions. PE was selected since it can be easily detached from epoxy resin. Then, the membranes and the supporting rod were press-fitted in the mask which was subsequently mounted on one end of a polyvinyl­chloride (PVC) pipe. The system was then potted with epoxy resin. After curing, the PE mask was removed, and the procedure was repeated on the other extremity of the rod and membranes. The effective length of the PVDF membranes was 30 cm and the total external surface area amounted to 2.87 * 10-2 m2, as the external diameter of the membrane, consisting of the support external diameter plus the deposited polymeric layer amounted to 3.8 mm. An example of module constructed in this way is shown in Fig. 3.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: the support external diameter plus the deposited polymeric layer amounted to 3.8 mm. An example of module constructed in this way is shown in Fig. 3. The rig was built as in Fig. 4. The membrane module was mounted with two O-rings into a transparent PVC housing with four outlets (D), two for the liquid line on the outer membrane surface and two for the air flowing on the membrane lumen. The transparent housing was chosen to follow by visual observation of the biofilm growth on the outer mem­brane surface. A tank (A) was connected to a small centrifugal pump (Qmax: 240 L/h, Hmax: 3 mH2O) (B) which led to the module housing (D) or to a discharge drain (C). Downstream the module, a flow meter was placed (E). The air line was in counter current to the liquid line. The air entering the lumen of the membranes came from a pressure reducer (F), connected to a pressure gauge (G). At the exit from the lumen, an air flow regulation valve (H) and a bubble flow meter (I) were placed to measure the latter. The total volume for the wastewater of the MABR was 1350 mL. In this setup, a solution composed by 50% of the aforementioned suspended biomass and 50% of a nutrient solution was recirculated at 50 L/h on the outer side of the hollow fibre membranes to stimulate the biofilm formation. The nutrient solution was prepared following liter­ature data [28] in order to imitate a municipal wastewater, in which organic substances and inorganic salts can be found. Specifically:

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: Meat extract + peptone (ratio 3:5 w/w by Difco Laboratories) 340 ppm Urea (Sigma Aldrich) 20 ppm CaCl2* 2H2O (Alpha Aesar) 4 ppm MgSO4 *7H2O (Carlo Erba) 2 ppm K2HPO4 (Merck) 11 ppm NaCl (Don Jerez) 7 ppm (NH4)2CO3 (Acros organics) 20 ppm The air stream was fed in the lumen side of the fibres at 250 mL/min and an inlet relative pressure of 25 mbar. For seeding the biomass on the membrane surface, the biomass was recirculated for 48 h on the membrane and then discharged. The adhesion of the biomass to the fibres was verified through a visual evaluation of the appearance evolution of the external surface of the membranes. Afterwards, the biomass was regularly fed with the nutrient solution and its growth was monitored, determining the nutrients con­centration reduction over time. Some studies have shown that there is a certain correlation between COD and the absorbance at 220 nm in the UV spectrum, caused by the absorption of ultraviolet radiation of organic substances in this area. However, to mask the interference due to UV absorption by nitrates, it is also possible to measure absorbance at 250 nm [29]. It has been demonstrated that the method for measuring COD through absorbance at 220 nm has good linearity and is correlated to the results obtained with the method that uses potassium permanganate as an oxidizing agent, but, in addition, it is a simple and fast analysis approach [30].

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: lts obtained with the method that uses potassium permanganate as an oxidizing agent, but, in addition, it is a simple and fast analysis approach [30]. The instrument used was the Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer equip­ped with quartz cuvettes. The calibration was obtained by analysing several samples with a traditional COD determination cuvette (Lovibond kit COD LR/25 2420720) and the correlated Lovibond MD 200 spectrophotometer. The analyses with the kit were repeated when the composition of the solution of nutrients was changed. The reduction of ammoniacal nitrogen concentration, which is indicative of the nitrification mechanism, and of the total nitrogen related to the denitrification process, were evaluated using the specific kits from Merck Millipore (Spectroquant Ammonium Test 1.00683 and Spectroquant Nitrogen Cell Test 1.14537, respectively). For the determination of nitrites and nitrates, products of ammonium oxidation and harmful to the health of biomass, anion exchange chro­matography was used. Samples for analysis were filtered with a 0.2 μm cellulose acetate syringe filter to remove any turbidity present. The in­strument for the determination was the Dionex DX-120 equipped with an IonPac AS9-HC column and using a 0.96 g/L Na2CO3 solution as mobile phase.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

Fig. 3. Membrane module built for MABR application.

Fig. 4. MABR rig set up scheme. A: feed tank, B: centrifugal pump, C: feed drain valve, D: module, E: liquid flowmeter, F: air pressure reducer, G: air pressure gauge, H: air flow regulation valve, I: bubble flowmeter.

F. Passaro et al. Journal of Membrane Science 745 (2026) 125227

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: 3. Results 3.1. Rheology and membrane properties The rheology of the polymeric solution is an important factor to consider when preparing polymeric membranes. The temperature in­fluences the dope solution viscosity, which in turn affects how it spreads onto the substrate for forming the polymeric membrane. Moreover, the viscosity affects the precipitation process in the NIPS technique. Eval­uating viscosity as the temperature changes, allows to better understand the final characteristics of the PVDF membranes produced. Fig. 5 shows the effect of temperature on the viscosity of the PVDF solution and the influence of the shear rate on the shear stress during the measurements. As expected, increasing the solution temperature resulted in a steep decrease of the solution viscosity that dropped from around 1430 cP at 30 ◦C, down to 570 cP at 70 ◦C (Fig. 5A). Since viscosity has a major impact on both the dip coating [31] and the nonsolvent induced phase separation [32] processes, it was decided to prepare the membranes in this temperature range. In the observed speed range (Fig. 5B), the shear stress was always proportional to the shear rate. Since the intercept of the regression line was almost null, the correlation between the shear stress and shear rate of the solution follows the Newtonian viscosity law equation. PVDF membranes obtained were weighed and their thickness measured with a calliper. Fig. 6 shows the membrane weight per unit length as a function of viscosity.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: ranes obtained were weighed and their thickness measured with a calliper. Fig. 6 shows the membrane weight per unit length as a function of viscosity. The solution viscosity has a clear effect on the amount of polymer that is deposited on the braided support. This influence is generally more visible at lower temperatures (30 and 40 ◦C) while it is more subtle for the samples prepared at higher temperatures. An increase in viscosity means that the polymer solution takes longer to run off the substrate during deposition. It can be observed that, for all polymer solution temperatures, a higher withdrawal rate results in a greater amount of polymer being deposited. The dependency is more evident at lower temperatures, when the viscosity of the solution is higher. With a faster withdrawal rate, less time elapses between the start of deposition on the substrate and the moment when the membrane is placed in the vessel with the nonsolvent and phase separation begins. This, combined with a lower viscosity, causes a greater polymer buildup. The effect of speed seems more relevant than the temperature of the polymer solution. The increase of the weight of the polymer coating results in an in­crease of the membrane thickness which depends on both the with­drawal speed and temperature, as reported in Fig. 7. In general, faster withdrawal speeds result in a thicker polymeric layer deposited on the braided support.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: eed and temperature, as reported in Fig. 7. In general, faster withdrawal speeds result in a thicker polymeric layer deposited on the braided support. This trend is more pronounced for membranes produced at lower temperatures, especially those pro­duced at 30 ◦C, 40 ◦C and 50 ◦C, while for the membranes produced at 60 ◦C and 70 ◦C, the effect is less relevant. Since the solutions had a Newtonian behaviour in the dip coating conditions, the Landau-Levich model can be used to describe the film formation [33,34]. When a plate is immersed in a liquid bath, a static

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: F. Passaro et al. Journal of Membrane Science 745 (2026) 125227 meniscus forms in the contact area between the plate and the liquid. The liquid adheres to the surface of the plate due to surface tension, while gravity acts on the surface of the liquid. Both forces act in the contact zone and a static meniscus is formed. This zone is characterized by a capillary length that is proportional to the surface tension and inversely proportional to the density of the liquid and the force of gravity. When the plate is pulled out of the liquid, at a low and constant speed, a drag of liquid is observed, which is deposited on the plate as a flat film. This phenomenon depends on the viscosity of the fluid, as the molecules near the plate adhere to it and, due to the viscous effect, drag the neighbouring molecules with them. The dragging causes a defor­mation of the static meniscus, which becomes a dynamic meniscus, whose characteristic length is assumed to be shorter than that of the static meniscus, as the effect of surface tension on gravity is assumed to predominate. Continuing to extract the plate, a layer of fluid will form, whose thickness can be estimated with the following equation. h0 = ki ( EL⋅u + D⋅u2 / )Eq.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: ontinuing to extract the plate, a layer of fluid will form, whose thickness can be estimated with the following equation. h0 = ki ( EL⋅u + D⋅u2 / )Eq. 1 where h0 is the thickness, ki is a constant that depends on the compo­sition of the solution, u is the extraction rate from the solution, E is the evaporation rate, L is the width of the substrate and D is a constant that considers the chemical and physical characteristics of the solution. According to this model, the variation of thickness with extraction rate has a V-shaped pattern, depending on whether the viscous effect (represented by the term Du 2/3)) or the surface tension effect (represented by the term E/(Lu)) predominates. An example of this trend is shown in Fig. 8.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: There are three zones that can be distinguished in this process. The first zone is characterized by an increase in thickness with the with­drawal speed (viscous drag regime, blue zone in Fig. 8), while the second zone shows a decrease in thickness with speed (capillary regime, green in Fig. 8). In the intermediate zone, both these phenomena are present to different extents and correspond to the minimum thickness of the coated layer. At low withdrawal speeds, the film thickness is influenced by the interaction between the continuous evaporation of the solvent at the meniscus and the capillary rise at the drying line. On the other hand, at high speeds, the final thickness is primarily determined by viscous resistance. When it comes to intermediate speeds, both phenomena overlap, and there is a critical speed at which the film reaches its min­imum value [35]. The Landau-Levich model can be partially applied to the braided PVDF membranes prepared for this work. The trend in polymer layer thickness as a function of withdrawal speed (Fig. 7) appears to be influenced by both the intermediate regime accounting both capillary and viscous contribution at the lowest experimental speeds used, and by the viscous regime at the highest speeds. The observed pattern seems less comparable at higher temperatures (60 ◦C and 70 ◦C).

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: tal speeds used, and by the viscous regime at the highest speeds. The observed pattern seems less comparable at higher temperatures (60 ◦C and 70 ◦C). This could be due to the low viscosity of the solution at these temperatures, causing the solution to run down the substrate before the ethanol phase separation and precipitation are triggered. As a result, the layer tends to be thinner and more homogeneous but shows additional deviation with the Landau-Levich model even though the correlation was enough. Additional considerations on the Landau-Levich model, an attempt of linearization to verify experimental data fitting and a multivariate

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: F. Passaro et al. Journal of Membrane Science 745 (2026) 125227 analysis with the Principal Component Analysis to support the correla­tion of the thickness with the withdrawal speed are reported in the Supplementary section. 3.2. Membrane properties Liquid displacement porometry was used directly on prepared PVDF membranes to determine the influence of the temperature of the poly­mer solution and the withdrawal speed of the polymer solution on the average pore diameter. Fig. 9 shows in a graph the average pore diam­eter of the membrane as a function of viscosity and thus the temperature of the polymer solution. At all withdrawal speeds, the membranes produced at the lowest temperature (30 ◦C) and highest viscosity have an average pore diam­eter of about 0.1 μm, which is lower than that of every other membrane prepared in this work. However, the variation of pore diameter with speed at this temperature remains within the experimental error range indicated by the error bars. The dependence on the withdrawal speed is more pronounced at higher temperatures (50 ◦C and 70 ◦C). In particular, the membranes produced at higher speeds have a larger mean pore size than those produced at lower speeds and those produced at lower temperatures. The largest value for the average pore diameter is around 0.6 μm. This effect is also visible in the FE-SEM images of the membrane surface, reported in Fig. 10. The membranes prepared at lower withdrawal speeds (A, C, E, G in Fig.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: also visible in the FE-SEM images of the membrane surface, reported in Fig. 10. The membranes prepared at lower withdrawal speeds (A, C, E, G in Fig. 10) and low temperature (B in Fig. 10) have a similar structure, with polymeric domains interconnected by thin fibrils. Withdrawal speed does not appear to be the controlling variable for the membrane surface structure at 30 ◦C. On the other hand, the samples prepared at higher speed and higher temperatures (D, F, H, J and partially I in Fig. 10) are characterized by a completely different surface structure that is composed of aggregated spherulitic domains, more prominently for membranes prepared at 60 ◦C. So, the combination of lower solution viscosity and higher withdrawal speed has a major impact on the for­mation of spherulites. Besides, the membranes prepared at 70 ◦C have an irregular structure, with some defects on the surface. The different surface structures had also an impact on the hydrophobicity of the membranes as confirmed by the water contact angle reported in Fig. 11. It can be noted that contact angle is higher than 90◦for all the membrane samples, so the membranes exhibit a marked hydrophobic character. However, there are some differences which can be linked to withdrawal speed of the dip coating and viscosity of the dope solution. This is highlighted also by comparing the values with the FESEM images of Fig. 10.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: drawal speed of the dip coating and viscosity of the dope solution. This is highlighted also by comparing the values with the FESEM images of Fig. 10. A lesser porous structure, such as in membrane 30_0.5 and 30_1.6, shows both a lower mean pore size and contact angle, because lower surface porosity creates more contact surface between the water droplet and the membrane, subsequently lowering the contact angle. Instead, with membranes with higher surface porosity, the water droplet is able to enter the so-called fakir state, and the resulting contact angle is higher. This effect is enhanced by the formation of a spherulitic structure as seen in sample 60_1.6 (Fig. 10H) and leads to the higher water contact angle. A minor tendency to spherulites formation can be found also in membranes 60_0.5, 40_1.6 and 50_1.6, which are characterized by a higher water contact angle compared to other membranes. The mem­branes prepared at 70 ◦C also show a higher contact angle value, but the variability of the data is affected by the presence of defects on the surface. Fig. 12 shows the cross-section structure of a membrane near the external surface (A) and at the interface with the braided support (B). As it was observed for flat sheet membranes in previous works [23], the use of a weak nonsolvent like ethanol generates symmetric mem­brane structures.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: s it was observed for flat sheet membranes in previous works [23], the use of a weak nonsolvent like ethanol generates symmetric mem­brane structures. Both the external surface (A) and the inner layer are characterized by a similar morphology composed by interconnected cavities of similar dimensions. Fig. 12B represents the cross section of the membrane in corre­spondence with the braided support. During the sample preparation, that involved the fragile breakage of the membrane in liquid nitrogen, the glass fibre did not break but detached from the polymeric matrix, leaving its imprint. During the dip coating process, the polymeric solu­tion adheres to the woven and only partially penetrates the sheath structure. Since the final purpose of the preparation of PVDF hollow fibre membranes was to determine the best working condition for them to act as support for biofilm growth in a MABR set up, the optimal membrane needed to be as thin as possible, to oppose the smallest resistance to the mass transport of the air from the lumen to the outside. For this reason, the membranes selected were the ones prepared through dip coating using a low viscosity solution and low withdrawal speeds rates. In the Supplementary section, a brief consideration on the use of multivariate analysis to confirm the influence of the two main param­eters, withdrawal speed and solution temperature, on the thickness and the pore size of the membranes.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: to confirm the influence of the two main param­eters, withdrawal speed and solution temperature, on the thickness and the pore size of the membranes. Moreover, in the Supplementary, a Fourier transformer infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum is reported, with some considerations on the crystalline phases of the PVDF in the membranes.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: MABR setting were of the 60_0.5 group. It was decided to use mem­branes prepared at 60 ◦C rather than 70 ◦C because these last ones generally showed more macroscopic defects on the external surface, since during the NIPS, the low viscosity of the solution at this temper­ature caused some portion of the membrane to be stretched outwards as the solvent and non-solvent mixed together. Biomass growth was monitored over time by visual observation of the degree of biofilm coverage of the membranes and the reduction of COD. Extracellular polymeric material began to be visible about twenty days after the end of the inoculation period, and it reached almost total coverage of the sur­face after a month and a half. In Fig. 13A the cross section of a PVDF fibre membrane with 2 months old biofilm is shown. The upper zone of the picture shows the sharp interface between the polymer membrane layer (orange arrow, at the top), as seen previously in Fig. 12, and the biofilm matrix (green arrow, at the bottom), in which sections of various elongated bacterial cells of different species, immersed in a complex matrix, are visible. In Fig. 13B a FESEM image of the surface of the biofilm layer is shown, where various spheroidal cells or spheroidal vacancies can be seen, as well as a homogeneous matrix engulfing them.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: ace of the biofilm layer is shown, where various spheroidal cells or spheroidal vacancies can be seen, as well as a homogeneous matrix engulfing them. The matrix of the biofilm is made of various substances (poly­saccharides, proteins, lipids) that together take part of the extracellular polymeric substance (EPS), a complex structure secreted by microor­ganisms, which provide a stable structure to the bacterial cells and protects them from external agent such as liquid shear stress, but also from chemical poisoning [36,37]. The biofilm mean thickness reached about 50 μm and it was uniform

content: F. Passaro et al. Journal of Membrane Science 745 (2026) 125227 in all the membrane surface. The analyses made over time on the synthetic wastewater showed that, after biofilm maturity was reached, this MABR set up could abate different kinds of pollutants, whose removal kinetics require different oxygen concentrations, since oxygen molecule acts as electron acceptor in some of the enzymatic reactions, whereas other reactions require different electron acceptors. The pollutants abated were organic substances, ammoniacal nitro­gen and total nitrogen. The firsts need oxygen to be oxidized, while total nitrogen requires an anaerobic environment and organic carbon as electron donor. Being able to carry out these different mechanisms means having different conditions in the same reactor, so, since the only biomass in the MABR was attached to the surface of the membranes, the biofilm mass transfer model could be schematized as in Fig. 14. On the membrane side, the concentration of oxygen, being closer to membrane lumen, was high, while organic carbon source and ammonia were consumed by the oxidating process. On the outer region of the biofilm, where denitrification happened, the condition was anoxic and the carbon source concentration was similar to the one in the bulk. Different conditions allowed this system to differentiate biomass species and promote their development and simultaneously abate multiple pollutants.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: Different conditions allowed this system to differentiate biomass species and promote their development and simultaneously abate multiple pollutants. The reduction of COD, ammoniacal nitrogen and total nitrogen achieved by this plant 2 months after the end of the inoculation period is reported in Fig. 15. Working with a high COD/ammonium ratio seemed to inhibit the initial reduction of ammonium in favour of that of COD. Furthermore, after 48 h the system entered an endogenous phase (shown as an increment of the COD concentration that is caused by the content of dead cells in the aqueous environment). The removal achieved with this type of plant is shown in Table 1. Due to an endogenous phase, the COD reduction recorded after 72 h was very low (14%). However, considering only the first 48 h, the COD abatement raised to 78%. The denitrification mechanism, shown as a decrease of total nitrogen concentration, although present, could be inhibited due to the death of the microorganisms responsible for the anaerobic reduction of oxidized nitrogenous compounds to gaseous nitrogen. These organisms are generally located on the external part of the biofilm, in which oxygen is present at a lower concentration, but where oxidized compounds are closer to the liquid bulk concentration. However, from the data in Fig. 15, denitrification, still proceeds after 48 h. After 72 h the con­centration of total nitrogen is about 164 ppm.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: tion. However, from the data in Fig. 15, denitrification, still proceeds after 48 h. After 72 h the con­centration of total nitrogen is about 164 ppm. In this MABR, increasing the hydraulic retention time seems to in­crease the removal rate for total nitrogen, at the expense of COD reduction. On the other hand, the 48 h target seems ideal to remove a major quantity of organic substances, measured as COD. In this setup, the biofilm thickness could not be sufficient to favour the presence of an anoxic zone, in which denitrifying bacteria grow and abate total nitro­gen. Biofilm thickness, previously shown in Fig. 13, is about 50 μm,

content: F. Passaro et al. Journal of Membrane Science 745 (2026) 125227 whereas in other studies it has been shown that biofilm thickness up to about 350 μm allows for an improved total nitrogen removal. A further developing of this MABR rig biological system could help improving simultaneous reduction of COD and total nitrogen. The specific removal rates of the setup described in our work are coherent with other researchers’ results. Wang et al. state specific removal rates of 4.78 g/(m2day) for COD, 2.15 g/(m2day) for ammonia and 1.25 g/(m2day) for total nitrogen for a single-step pilot-scale MABR [38]. In another study from Yang et al., an MABR with plate membranes (PMABR) achieved specific removal rates of 30.09 ± 0.91 g/m2day, with a similar initial load of COD, but using a higher transmembrane pressure (up to 80 mbar) and a lower hydraulic retention time (10 h) [39]. In other studies, some commercial PVDF membranes are used. Abdelfattah et al., investigated the abatements with a higher trans­membrane pressure of air and a similar COD initial load, leading to specific removal rates of about 6.8 g/(m2day) for COD and 0.47 g/(m2day) for ammonia, when the system was at a steady-state [40]. Table 2 shows all these systems compared together. In the current work, the MABR rig has been operated in batch configuration, and the residence time was not optimized. 4.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: se systems compared together. In the current work, the MABR rig has been operated in batch configuration, and the residence time was not optimized. 4. Conclusion Hollow fibre PVDF membranes were successfully prepared through immersion of a braided support in a 14.5 wt% polymeric solution of PVDF in TEP and consequent precipitation via immersion in ethanol 96 v %. By varying the deposition conditions such as the withdrawal speed during the dip-coating process and the temperature of the polymer so­lution, it was possible to modulate the thickness of the polymeric layer and the size of the pores. The thickness of the membranes seems to follow the trends predicted by the Landau-Levich theory. For various temperatures, the behaviour appears to be predomi­nantly in the viscosity-governed regime, where an increase in thickness is achieved as the extraction speed increases. Instead, the effect of temperature on the final thickness is not as relevant. The temperature has a significant impact on the viscosity of the PVDF solution and, furthermore, influences the pore formation mechanism. The prepared membranes were sufficiently uniform, and the use of the glass fibre support gave them high mechanical stability making them suitable for numerous applications.

citation.title: Preparation of hollow fibre braided polyvinylidene fluoride membranes by dip coating for membrane aerated bioreactor

content: were sufficiently uniform, and the use of the glass fibre support gave them high mechanical stability making them suitable for numerous applications. The MABR tests highlighted adequate oxygen transfer and biofilm adhesion and growth, and the analyses confirmed biofilm stratification of different working conditions, justifying simultaneous abatements of multiple pollutants on a single reactor. When the biofilm was sufficiently formed, it was able to remove effectively 78% of the COD in 48 h and 41% of ammoniacal nitrogen and 11% of total nitrogen in 72 h. CRediT authorship contribution statement Francesca Passaro: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Investigation. Marcello Pagliero: Writing – original draft, Visualiza­tion, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ilaria Rizzardi: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Antonio Comite: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.
